comparisons.kpi_name: Does the company have an AI strategy?
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Formal governance structure
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Orange S.A. operates a formal AI governance framework comprising: (1) an independent Data & AI Ethics Council reporting to the Executive Committee that defines the Group’s ethical framework for data/AI, oversees use cases, and monitors deployment of the Data & AI Ethical Charter; (2) a Responsible AI Design Authority that classifies the risk level of each AI project and a centralized Responsible AI Committee that decides on complex deployment, accountability and risk issues; (3) a secure internal AI platform providing centralized governance, integrated GDPR safeguards and end‑to‑end lifecycle support under a “Responsible AI by Design” approach with embedded human oversight and intervention mechanisms; and (4) enterprise risk and board‑level oversight via the Group Risks Committee and the Board’s Audit Committee (risk management and controls), Strategy & Technology Committee (technology strategy) and Governance & Corporate Social and Environmental Responsibility Committee (charters and compliance programs). The company also aligns with the EU AI Act through risk assessment/mitigation and regular audits and participates in the EU AI Pact.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: This summary consolidates Orange’s cited AI oversight bodies and mechanisms: the Data & AI Ethics Council (independent, accountable to the Executive Committee, publishes and oversees the Data & AI Ethical Charter) [1][12]; the Responsible AI Design Authority and a centralized Responsible AI Committee for AI risk qualification and deployment/accountability decisions [2]; a secure internal AI platform with centralized governance and GDPR safeguards, plus a “Responsible AI by Design” approach with human oversight across the lifecycle [2]; risk oversight via the Risks Committee and board committees—Audit (risk management/control effectiveness), Strategy & Technology (strategic/technological orientations), and Governance & CSER (charters and compliance programs) [3][5]. Proactive EU AI Act alignment, including risk management planning and regular audits, and participation in the EU AI Pact are also stated by Orange [2].

comparisons.kpi_name: Percentage of AI initiatives with outcomes tied to measurable business KPIs.
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI use cases with quantitative impact since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Five AI use cases announced or updated since the beginning of 2023 report a clear, numeric impact.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: A review of Orange S.A. (Orange) press releases, blog posts, and conference presentations dated 2024–2025 identified five distinct use cases that each publish at least one hard number describing the outcome: (1) a retail-sector sales-forecasting model that doubled the flagship store’s revenue within months; (2) a banking recommendation engine that lifted cross-sales by 35 %; (3) an industrial-IoT predictive-maintenance system that saved several million euros in its first year; (4) Orange’s own mobile-antenna energy-efficiency optimiser that has cut energy consumption by about 5 %; and (5) an e-commerce product-categorisation pipeline that now classifies 85 % of items automatically, leaving only 15 % for GenAI-assisted human review. Because all five were first disclosed (or materially updated) after 1 January 2023 and each comes with an explicit quantitative result—whether financial, cost, or accuracy—they are counted here.

comparisons.kpi_name: Presence of AI talent/experience on the Board
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI talents
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: A strict deduplication and classification by AI talent type were performed using job titles and roles, only including those who are verifiably current AI professionals at Orange S.A. or its group entities. 125 unique, current AI talents were found that meet the defined criteria for 'AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software Implementation, and AI Product Management' based on their validated roles and employment status in the research set.

comparisons.kpi_name: Number of use cases  and alignment with strategic priorities
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI use cases with financial impact since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Four AI use cases disclosed since 2023 include an explicit financial outcome.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Among the five quantitatively-reported use cases found since 2023, four provide a money-related figure—either revenue uplift or cost reduction—and therefore qualify as cases with a ‘financial impact’: (1) the retail sales-forecasting model (revenue doubled); (2) the banking recommendation engine (35 % cross-sales increase); (3) the industrial predictive-maintenance deployment (multi-million-euro savings in year one); and (4) Orange’s network energy-efficiency optimiser (energy-cost savings linked to a 5 % consumption drop). The e-commerce product-categorisation solution, while reporting an 85 % accuracy figure, does not translate that metric into an economic value and is therefore excluded from the financial-impact count, leaving the total at four.

comparisons.kpi_name: When were the first AI solutions executed?
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI related acquisitions since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: After strict validation of the 15 provided research items, only three entities—InstaDeep (acquired 2023), Xperis (acquired 2023 by Enovacom, an Orange Business subsidiary), and Expertime (acquired 2023 by Orange Business)—qualify as direct AI-related acquisitions by Orange S.A. since 2023. Partnerships (e.g. Mistral AI, OpenAI, JUMO), investments (Supervizor, DataGalaxy), joint ventures (Aduna), and indirect relationships via vendor partnerships (Run:ai, NVIDIA) do not meet the definition of Orange S.A. acquiring a company for AI capabilities. Océan is excluded as Orange S.A. was the seller, not the buyer. Other listed items either do not represent acquisitions by Orange S.A. or did not qualify as direct AI asset/capability acquisitions. Evidence comes from Orange's official newsroom, press releases, and SEC filings indicating completed AI-centric acquisitions.

comparisons.kpi_name: Investments in AI-focused companies
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI investments since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: We identified 38 unique AI-related investments or partnerships made by Orange S.A. or its dedicated investment arms (including Orange Ventures, Orange Digital Investment, and Orange Collective) since 2023. These include direct investments in AI startups (e.g., DataGalaxy, Squirro, Supervizor, Ringover, Pennylane, Epoca, Moments Lab, Actility, Terragon, ToumAI, Nucleon Security, Chari, Rofim, etc.), acquisitions (NEHS Digital, Xperis), partnerships for joint AI development (e.g., OpenAI, Mistral AI, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, AWS, Blue Planet/Ciena, Edarat Group, IBM, Perplexity, Palo Alto Networks, Fortinet, Cisco, Artefact, Splunk, Elastic), and establishment or expansion of AI-enabled products/offers (Live Intelligence, 5G GenAI Assistant, AI & GenAI Scale Offer, CSG Quote & Order). All entities were strictly validated for investment type, AI relation, direct Orange S.A. tie, and timeline (since Jan 2023), and duplicates or commercial-only relationships were excluded as required by the data point definition.

comparisons.kpi_name: Partnerships with AI companies
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Name of partnerships
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Sappi; NVIDIA; Mistral AI; Nozomi Networks; Qualtrics; SAS; SITA; Imagino; NTT; Riverbed; Liferay; University of Grenoble Alpes/CNRS (Telecom4Health); Thales; Dell Technologies; Samsung; Institut Curie; Artefact; ServiceNow; Qevlar AI; Vodafone; Cloudera; Capgemini (Bleu); AWS; Teradata; Blue Planet (Ciena); SentinelOne; ThousandEyes; DataGalaxy; Red Hat; TM Forum; Fortinet; Salesforce; ML6; HPE; Wind River; LightOn; Microsoft; Nokia; Huawei; Ekinops; Cisco; BMC Helix; Institut Polytechnique de Paris (IP Paris); ILLUIN Technology; Siemens; Elastic; ITU-T; Apache Spark (Orange3-Spark); Neo4j; Varonis; UiPath; Supervizor; Netskope; Klaxoon; Nuabee; Amdocs; Snowflake; Meta; EmailTree; Splunk; Atos; NICE; Rubrik; Genesys; Inria (I/O Lab); O-RAN SC/Kubeflow; Qlik; SecBI; Famoco; Juniper Networks; Oracle; GAIA-X; GSMA; 6G-PATH; Kyndryl; CGI; CrowdStrike; BuyIn (Deutsche Telekom); Mavenir; Future4care; Orange Fab France/Brainbox AI; MobileIron; Tableau; Qualys; Pega; MASORANGE/MasMovil; MasOrange/Microsoft Copilot
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: I compiled a deduplicated list of all organizations confirmed to have official AI-related partnerships with Orange S.A. or its subsidiaries. Only entities with clear evidence of an AI focus and an official relationship were included. Entities with ambiguous or non-AI scope, or without verified links, were excluded. All names were normalized to organization level per the definition, and duplicate partnerships (e.g., same entity across multiple Orange subsidiaries) were unified under their canonical name. The list reflects all unique partnerships meeting the data point criteria.

comparisons.kpi_name: Job postings- number of open positions advertised in the public domain
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of publicly advertised in HR/job postings?
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: After reviewing all 58 research entities, 51 unique AI-related HR/job postings at Orange S.A. (including Orange Business, Orange Group, and acquired/owned entities like Business & Decision and Orange Logic) directly fit the data point definition: public, company-affiliated, and AI-maturity-relevant. These include roles in data & AI engineering, AI strategy, AI ethics, AI consulting, AI research, and digital/data transformation, each cited in validated job or career portals of Orange and its subsidiaries (deduped by unique posting and role). Duplicates and ambiguous items were excluded; only current, directly advertised jobs relevant to AI maturity were counted.

comparisons.kpi_name: AI Team Leadership structure- number of leaders (percentage of Job Titles)
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of Board members with AI experience
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: A thorough review of Orange S.A.'s Board of Directors composition, as detailed in the 2024-2025 Integrated Annual Report and governance disclosures, confirms that none of the 15 current board members possess proven AI expertise. While some directors have extensive backgrounds in technology (e.g., Valérie Beaulieu at Microsoft, Gilles Grapinet in IT services), there is no evidence that any board member has deep, documented experience specifically in artificial intelligence or machine learning. The company relies on an external Data and AI Ethics Council for specialized AI oversight, but its members are not part of the Board. This is consistent with the KPI reasoning, which states that the board meets the committee oversight requirement but not the threshold for directors with proven AI expertise.

comparisons.kpi_name: AI Team Leadership structure- number of leaders (in number)
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of data breaches in past 5 years
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Based on the provided analysis and supporting claims, Orange S.A. and its subsidiaries experienced at least six distinct, documented data breaches in the past five years (2020–2025): 1) Orange Business Solutions (Nefilim ransomware, July 2020), 2) Orange Belgium (July 2025), 3) Orange Romania (HellCat/Rey, February 2025), 4) Orange Group France (Warlock ransomware, August 2025), 5) Orange Spain (RIPE account hijack, February 2025), and 6) Orange Group France (July 2025, operational disruption—though no data exfiltration confirmed, it is still counted as a major incident per the definition). Older breaches from 2014 are outside the five-year window. This count is strictly based on the incidents described in the claims and their timing.

comparisons.kpi_name: AI Ethics role appointed at company, AI risk and management
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: AI Ethics documentation
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Orange S.A. has formal AI ethics documentation: its Data & AI Ethics Charter (published in Nov 2022) is deployed group-wide and sets an overarching framework for ethical, responsible and transparent use of data and AI. This is operationalized through a “Responsible AI by Design” governance approach and overseen by dedicated bodies—the Data & AI Ethics Council, a Responsible AI Design Authority, and a centralized Responsible AI Committee—that cover risk classification, human oversight, bias mitigation, and alignment with the EU AI Act.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Orange explicitly states it created and published a Data & AI Ethics Charter in November 2022, to be deployed across the Group [9][21][18]. It also details a “Responsible AI by Design” philosophy and formal governance with a Responsible AI Design Authority and a centralized Responsible AI Committee to classify risks and ensure ethical use, alongside the Data & AI Ethics Council [2]. Orange further notes alignment of its practices with the EU AI Act and its own Charter [16][2].

comparisons.kpi_name: Compliance with AI regulations
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: workplace reduction in FTEs
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: 0% (no reported FTE reduction attributed to AI/automation)
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Despite a 7.43% year-over-year reduction in total workforce and a 30% reduction in French telecoms activities since 2018, Orange S.A. has explicitly stated that these reductions are not attributable to automation or AI adoption. Company leadership, including the CEO, has repeatedly emphasized that AI-driven efficiencies are not resulting in headcount reductions, and no official figures link FTE reductions directly to AI or automation. Instead, reductions are due to voluntary programs, restructuring, and infrastructure changes. Therefore, the indicative workforce reduction in FTEs explicitly attributed to AI/automation is 0%.

comparisons.kpi_name: Partnership with AI Ethics organizations like IAPP
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: AI Ethics documentation
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Orange S.A. has formal AI ethics documentation: its Data & AI Ethics Charter (published in Nov 2022) is deployed group-wide and sets an overarching framework for ethical, responsible and transparent use of data and AI. This is operationalized through a “Responsible AI by Design” governance approach and overseen by dedicated bodies—the Data & AI Ethics Council, a Responsible AI Design Authority, and a centralized Responsible AI Committee—that cover risk classification, human oversight, bias mitigation, and alignment with the EU AI Act.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Orange explicitly states it created and published a Data & AI Ethics Charter in November 2022, to be deployed across the Group [9][21][18]. It also details a “Responsible AI by Design” philosophy and formal governance with a Responsible AI Design Authority and a centralized Responsible AI Committee to classify risks and ensure ethical use, alongside the Data & AI Ethics Council [2]. Orange further notes alignment of its practices with the EU AI Act and its own Charter [16][2].

comparisons.kpi_name: Governance documents published on AI deployment
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: First year of AI deployment
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: 2020 — By 14 August 2020, Orange publicly disclosed operational AI in production, using AI to detect abnormal network behavior and trigger automated remediation, and deploying chatbots and robotic process automation (RPA) at Orange Bank and across consumer and B2B mobile operations.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: The earliest dated, public evidence in the claims is a 14 August 2020 Orange Business page stating that AI detects abnormal network behavior and triggers automation, and that chatbots and RPA are used at Orange Bank and in the consumer/B2B mobile businesses—indicating operational-scale deployment by that year [26].

unmatched_kpis: C-level statements about AI Strategy
AI communications in social media, white papers (volume and boldness of messaging), limit to G7 or primary markets
Presence and clarity of a publicly communicated AI roadmap outlining long-term objectives, milestones, and investment priorities.
To what extent does the organization allocate and scale financial, human, and technological resources to AI initiatives in a way that delivers measurable business outcomes?
Type of technology, AI model (include agentic AI) point in time implementation or agents Maturity of agentic workforce within the operating model.
Has implementation been primarily an in-house development or partnership model? In-house or outsource some risk, external expertise

To what extent has the company achieved measurable workforce efficiency through AI or GenAI — such as automation, task augmentation, or optimized hiring — resulting in stable or increased output with equal or fewer FTEs?
To what extent has the company operationalized AI — demonstrating tangible rollout and active usage of AI or GenAI in core business operations, workflows, or decision processes at scale?

To what extent has the company maintained data security and confidentiality in its AI operations — including the presence or absence of data breaches, leaks, or other security incidents over the past five years?
To what extent has the company established a mature, enterprise-wide data strategy and foundational architecture that effectively supports AI development and deployment?
To what extent does the company integrate and harmonize data from diverse internal and external sources to make it efficiently usable for AI and analytics applications?
To what extent is the company’s hardware and cloud infrastructure capable of handling expanding AI workloads with high responsiveness, scalability, and cost-efficiency?
To what extent has the company adopted modern, enterprise-grade analytics and AI platforms that streamline model development, deployment, monitoring, and lifecycle management?
To what extent are the company’s processes and tools for developing, deploying, monitoring, and improving ML models mature, automated, and aligned with production standards?
To what extent has the company implemented robust mechanisms that safeguard data against unauthorized access or misuse, ensuring compliance with privacy and security regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA)?
To what extent is the company modernizing or integrating legacy systems into its current data and AI architecture to eliminate bottlenecks and data silos?
To what extent does the company explore and adopt complementary frontier technologies (e.g., IoT, blockchain, edge computing, AR/VR, quantum computing) that enhance or expand AI capabilities?
Training- disclosed training efforts and breadth of the activity (public disclosure)

Job titles- number of employees in AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software implementation, AI product management (other roles to be discussed) - Experience description demonstrates AI skill base or focus (percentage of total employees)

Job titles- number of employees in AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software implementation, AI product management (other roles to be discussed) - Experience description demonstrates AI skill base or focus (in number)
What department “owns” AI strategy? Do they have a tech department that has its own Board member?
To what extent does the company actively support employee well-being and engagement throughout its AI transformation journey?
How effectively does the organization foster international and multicultural collaboration in AI development and deployment?
How strongly is an AI-driven and innovation-oriented mindset embedded in the company’s organizational culture?
How mature and effective are the company’s change management and communication practices in enabling AI adoption?
How well are cross-functional collaboration and governance structures established to coordinate AI initiatives across departments?
To what degree has the company built AI literacy and encouraged knowledge sharing across the workforce?
How ready is the company’s culture to embrace data-driven decision-making and continuous AI-enabled improvement?
How does the company integrate community engagement and social responsibility into its AI initiatives?
To what extent does the company promote an ethical culture and mechanisms for continuous improvement in AI practices?
Open source- overall engagement with open source development ecosystem, code repositories, open source projects (i.e. GitHub)
How explicitly does AI enhance the customer value proposition (e.g., personalization, NPS, retention) and tie to growth/margin outcomes?
Evidence that AI strategy targets disruptive differentiation (new AI‑native offerings, pricing models, or channels) beyond efficiency.
How clear are cross‑functional roles and responsibilities for AI (business, product, data, risk, legal) from idea to run?
How systematically are external benchmarks used to drive AI delivery performance (speed, quality, cost) and improvement targets?
What business‑visible monitoring exists for models in production (performance, drift, data quality, incidents) and who sees it?
Is there a standardized, auditable methodology to estimate and realize ROI for AI (ex‑ante and ex‑post)?
How well is delivery aligned with applicable AI/sector regulations during rollout and operation (e.g., DPIA, model cards, DSARs)?
How mature and reproducible is the model development workflow (from experimentation to CI/CD and release)?
Do users have a governed self‑service data & AI portal/marketplace (catalog, access control, usage analytics)?
Is there an enterprise‑wide canonical data model that underpins AI/analytics across domains?
How complete and enforced are data & model and policies/standards (quality, lineage, privacy, retention, access)?
To what extent is metadata (technical + business) captured and used to improve discovery, lineage, and MDM?
Is risk appetite for AI explicitly defined (by domain/use case) and used for approvals and guardrails?
How visible and concrete is the company’s public advocacy and action on responsible AI (AI‑for‑Good)?
Are role‑based learning paths and curricula established for AI (from execs to engineers) with certification?
How structured and scalable is experimentation (PoCs/pilots) with clear success metrics and governance?
Are there structured alliances/JVs to accelerate AI value with shared IP, data, or distribution?
Do standardized, privacy‑preserving data‑sharing mechanisms exist across the ecosystem?
Is the firm actively shaping AI standards and regulation via consortia, position papers, and pilots?
How rigorous is partner/vendor capability assessment (security, compliance, quality, delivery, integration)?
